content.content:
=== PLAN ===
Build a p-code VM and p-code assembler (pasm) for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA. The VM is a stack-based interpreter written in COR24 assembly. The pasm tool (also COR24 assembly) assembles human-readable .pasm source into bytecode for testing the VM. Pascal compiler is a separate project.

=== STEPS ===
  [x] 001-vm-scaffold [production]: Create pvm.s scaffold: COR24 entry point, VM state struct (pc, esp, csp, fp, ...
  [x] 002-vm-stack-ops [legacy]: Implement push/push_s/dup/drop/swap/over opcode handlers and sys PUTC (opcode 0x60 with id=1). Test by embedding hardcoded bytecode: push_s 72, sys 1, push_s 10, sys 1, halt — should print H-newline to UART.
  [x] 003-vm-arithmetic [legacy]: Implement add/sub/mul/div/mod/neg/and/or/xor/not/shl/shr and eq/ne/lt/le/gt/ge opcode handlers. Test with hardcoded bytecode: push 7, push 6, mul, sys PUTC should output ASCII '*' (42).
  [x] 004-vm-control-flow [legacy]: Implement jmp/jz/jnz opcode handlers. Test with hardcoded countdown loop: push 5, loop: dup, jz done, push_s 1, sub, jmp loop, done: halt.
  [x] 005-vm-frames [legacy]: Implement call/ret/enter/leave and loadl/storel/loada/storea opcode handlers. Build frame management: push/pop activation records on call stack, argument passing from eval stack to frame. Test with hardcoded bytecode for a factorial function.
  [x] 006-vm-memory [legacy]: Implement loadg/storeg/addrg/addrl and indirect load/store/loadb/storeb. Implement globals segment. Test with hardcoded bytecode: global counter, store/load/print.
  [x] 007-vm-nonlocal [legacy]: Implement loadn/storen for static link chain traversal (needed for Pascal nested procedures). Test with hardcoded 2-level nested procedure accessing outer local.
  [x] 008-vm-syscalls [legacy]: Implement remaining sys calls: HALT(0), GETC(2), LED(3), ALLOC(4), FREE(5). Bump allocator for heap. Test: LED toggle, UART echo, heap alloc/write/read.
  [x] 009-vm-traps [legacy]: Implement trap handling for all trap codes (div-zero, stack overflow/underflow, invalid opcode, invalid address, nil pointer). Print diagnostic to UART on trap. Test each trap condition.
  [x] 010-pasm-lexer [legacy]: Create pasm.s — p-code assembler in COR24 assembly. Start with lexer: read pasm source from UART, tokenize into token types (mnemonic, number, label, directive, newline, comment, eof). Use a token buffer in memory. Test by feeding simple pasm source and dumping token stream.
  [x] 011-pasm-parser [legacy]: Implement pasm parser: two-pass assembly. Pass 1: collect labels and procedure addresses, count globals/data sizes. Pass 2: emit bytecode. Handle .const/.global/.data/.proc/.end directives. Test by assembling a minimal program (push 42, halt) and verifying bytecode bytes.
  [ ] 012-pasm-integration [legacy]: Connect pasm output to VM input. Assemble pasm source into bytecode buffer, then execute. Test with hello.pasm (full puts procedure, data string, main calling puts). First end-to-end test. <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 013-test-suite [legacy]: Create full test suite: tests/t01-arith.pasm through t10-traps.pasm. Create demo.sh test harness with golden output comparison (following tf24a pattern). Create examples/hello.pasm, examples/blink.pasm, examples/echo.pasm.
  [ ] 014-hardening [legacy]: Fix bugs found by test suite. Add stack depth checking. Validate memory bounds. Clean up diagnostic output. Ensure all tests pass. Final documentation update.

=== CURRENT STEP: 012-pasm-integration [legacy] ===
Status: pending
Description: Connect pasm output to VM input. Assemble pasm source into bytecode buffer, then execute. Test with hello.pasm (full puts procedure, data string, main calling puts). First end-to-end test.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

input.prompt:
Explore the pv24a project at /Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a. I need to understand:

1. In pasm.s - how does the assembler currently work? Where does it read input from? Where does it write output (bytecode)? What memory addresses/segments does it use? Look at the end of pass 2 - what happens after assembly is complete?

2. In pvm.s - how does the VM currently work? Where does it read bytecode from? What memory addresses does it use for the bytecode/code segment? How is the VM initialized?

3. What's the memory map layout? Check docs/architecture.md and docs/design.md for memory layout details.

4. List all .spc files in the project (these are p-code assembler source files).

Be thorough - read the actual assembly code to understand the memory layout and I/O flow. Focus on understanding how to connect pasm output directly to VM input so they can run end-to-end.

1→# pv24a — Architecture
     2→
     3→## System Layers
     4→
     5→```
     6→┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
     7→│  Layer 4: Language Front-Ends       │  (future, separate projects)
     8→│  Pascal compiler → .pasm / bytecode │
     9→│  Lisp, BASIC, etc.                  │
    10→├─────────────────────────────────────┤
    11→│  Layer 3: Language-Specific Runtime  │  (future, separate projects)
    12→│  Pascal: new/dispose, writeln, sets │
    13→├─────────────────────────────────────┤
    14→│  Layer 2: Base Runtime Services     │  ← this project (runtime portion)
    15→│  Heap allocator, bounds checking,   │
    16→│  nil/div-zero traps, I/O stubs      │
    17→├─────────────────────────────────────┤
    18→│  Layer 1: P-Code VM                 │  ← this project (core)
    19→│  Stack machine interpreter          │
    20→│  Eval stack + call stack + globals  │
    21→│  ~30 opcodes, frame model, traps    │
    22→├─────────────────────────────────────┤
    23→│  Layer 0: Target Substrate          │  (existing, external)
    24→│  COR24 ISA, cor24-run assembler,    │
    25→│  emulator, FPGA hardware            │
    26→└─────────────────────────────────────┘
    27→```
    28→
    29→**This project (pv24a)** implements Layers 1 and 2, plus a p-code assembler
    30→tool for testing.
    31→
    32→## Component Map
    33→
    34→```
    35→pv24a/
    36→├── pvm.s              P-Code VM interpreter (Layer 1)
    37→├── runtime.s          Base runtime services (Layer 2)
    38→├── pasm.s             P-Code assembler tool
    39→├── tests/
    40→│   ├── t01-arith.pasm     Arithmetic tests
    41→│   ├── t02-stack.pasm     Stack operation tests
    42→│   ├── t03-branch.pasm    Control flow tests
    43→│   ├── t04-proc.pasm      Procedure call tests
    44→│   ├── t05-memory.pasm    Memory access tests
    45→│   ├── t06-uart.pasm      UART I/O tests
    46→│   ├── t07-led.pasm       LED tests
    47→│   ├── t08-recurse.pasm   Recursion tests
    48→│   ├── t09-heap.pasm      Heap allocation tests
    49→│   └── t10-traps.pasm     Error/trap tests
    50→├── examples/
    51→│   ├── hello.pasm         Hello World
    52→│   ├── blink.pasm         LED blink
    53→│   └── echo.pasm          UART echo
    54→├── docs/
    55→│   ├── prd.md
    56→│   ├── architecture.md
    57→│   ├── design.md
    58→│   └── research.txt
    59→└── demo.sh                Test harness script
    60→```
    61→
    62→## Memory Map (COR24 address space)
    63→
    64→```
    65→0x000000 ┌──────────────────────┐
    66→         │ COR24 boot / entry   │  cor24-run entry point
    67→         ├──────────────────────┤
    68→         │ P-Code VM code       │  pvm.s assembled COR24 instructions
    69→         │ (interpreter loop,   │
    70→         │  opcode dispatch,    │
    71→         │  runtime services)   │
    72→         ├──────────────────────┤
    73→         │ P-Code bytecode      │  loaded/assembled p-code program
    74→         │ (code segment)       │
    75→         ├──────────────────────┤
    76→         │ Constants / strings  │  literal pool
    77→         ├──────────────────────┤
    78→         │ Globals segment      │  p-code global variables
    79→         ├──────────────────────┤
    80→         │ Call stack            │  frames: ret-pc, dyn-link, static-link,
    81→         │ (grows upward)       │  proc-id, locals
    82→         ├──────────────────────┤
    83→         │ Eval stack            │  expression temporaries
    84→         │ (grows upward)       │  (separate from call stack)
    85→         ├──────────────────────┤
    86→         │ Free space            │
    87→         ├──────────────────────┤
    88→         │ Heap                  │  dynamic allocation (grows upward)
    89→         │ (bump allocator v1)  │
    90→         ├──────────────────────┤
    91→0x0FFFFF │ End of SRAM           │
    92→         ├──────────────────────┤
    93→0xFEEC00 │ EBR (3KB)             │  COR24 hardware stack (sp)
    94→         │                      │  Used as scratch / COR24-level stack
    95→0xFEF7FF └──────────────────────┘
    96→0xFF0100   UART data register
    97→0xFF0101   UART status register
    98→0xFF0200   LED port
    99→```
   100→
   101→## Register Allocation (COR24 → VM)
   102→
   103→The COR24 has very few registers. For the VM interpreter:
   104→
   105→| COR24 Reg | VM Role         | Notes |
   106→|-----------|-----------------|-------|
   107→| r0        | W (work/scratch)| General scratch, opcode dispatch |
   108→| r1        | VM state ptr    | Points to VM state struct in memory |
   109→| r2        | Scratch #2      | Secondary work register |
   110→| sp        | COR24 stack     | Hardware push/pop (EBR), used sparingly |
   111→| fp        | Memory base     | Used for indexed loads/stores |
   112→
   113→VM state (pc, esp, csp, gp, hp, etc.) lives in a memory struct because
   114→COR24 doesn't have enough registers to hold it all.
   115→
   116→## Execution Model
   117→
   118→### Interpreter Loop (fetch-decode-execute)
   119→
   120→```
   121→vm_loop:
   122→    ; fetch opcode byte from p-code[pc]
   123→    ; pc += 1
   124→    ; decode: index into dispatch table
   125→    ; jump to handler
   126→    ; handler executes, modifies VM state
   127→    ; jump back to vm_loop
   128→```
   129→
   130→### Dual Stack Architecture
   131→
   132→- **Eval stack**: expression temporaries, pushed/popped by arithmetic and
   133→  load/store instructions. Grows upward in SRAM.
   134→- **Call stack**: procedure activation records (frames). Grows upward in
   135→  a separate SRAM region. Each frame contains:
   136→  - Return PC (p-code address)
   137→  - Dynamic link (previous frame pointer)
   138→  - Static link (lexically enclosing frame, for nested scopes)
   139→  - Procedure ID (for debugger)
   140→  - Local variable slots
   141→
   142→Separate stacks make debugging much easier and prevent eval stack corruption
   143→from damaging frame data.
   144→
   145→### Opcode Encoding
   146→
   147→P-code instructions are variable-length byte sequences:
   148→
   149→| Format | Bytes | Example |
   150→|--------|-------|---------|
   151→| op     | 1     | `add`, `dup`, `ret` |
   152→| op imm8 | 2   | `push_small 42` |
   153→| op imm24 | 4  | `push 100000`, `jmp label` |
   154→| op u8 u8 | 3  | `loadl 2` (local at offset 2) |
   155→
   156→## I/O Model
   157→
   158→The VM provides two I/O paths:
   159→
   160→1. **sys instructions** — abstract device calls (portable)
   161→   - `sys PUTC` — write byte to output
   162→   - `sys GETC` — read byte from input
   163→   - `sys LED` — write LED state
   164→   - `sys HALT` — stop execution
   165→
   166→2. **Memory-mapped I/O** — direct UART/LED access via load/store
   167→   - Available but discouraged for portable p-code programs
   168→   - Runtime library wraps MMIO behind sys calls
   169→
   170→## Build & Test
   171→
   172→```bash
   173→# Assemble and run VM with a test program
   174→cor24-run --run pvm.s -u "$(cat tests/t01-arith.pasm)" --speed 0 -n 5000000
   175→
   176→# Or: assemble pasm source, then run VM with bytecode
   177→# (exact mechanism TBD — may use UART pipe or preloaded memory)
   178→
   179→# Interactive mode
   180→cor24-run --run pvm.s --terminal --echo
   181→
   182→# Regression test
   183→./demo.sh test
   184→```
   185→
   186→## Relationship to Other Projects
   187→
   188→| Project | Role | Location |
   189→|---------|------|----------|
   190→| cor24-rs/cor24-bin | COR24 assembler + emulator | ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs |
   191→| tf24a | Pattern reference (Forth in COR24 asm) | ~/github/sw-cli-tools/tf24a |
   192→| pv24a | **This project** — P-Code VM + pasm | ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a |
   193→| (future) | Pascal compiler → p-code | TBD separate project |
   194→| (future) | Web debugger UI (Yew/WASM) | TBD separate project |
   195→

1→# pv24a — Design Document
     2→
     3→## 1. VM State
     4→
     5→The p-code VM maintains the following state in a memory struct:
     6→
     7→```
     8→vm_state:
     9→    .word 0     ; pc   — p-code instruction pointer
    10→    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
    11→    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
    12→    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
    13→    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
    14→    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
    15→    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
    16→    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped
    17→    .word 0     ; trap_code — last trap reason
    18→```
    19→
    20→## 2. P-Code Instruction Set
    21→
    22→### 2.1 Stack / Constants
    23→
    24→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    25→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    26→| `push` imm24 | 4 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push 24-bit immediate |
    27→| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
    28→| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
    29→| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
    30→| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
    31→| `over` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a b a ) | Copy second to top |
    32→
    33→### 2.2 Arithmetic / Logic
    34→
    35→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    36→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    37→| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
    38→| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
    39→| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
    40→| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
    41→| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
    42→| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
    43→| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
    44→| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
    45→| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
    46→| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
    47→| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
    48→| `shr` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a>>n ) | Arithmetic shift right |
    49→
    50→### 2.3 Comparison
    51→
    52→All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):
    53→
    54→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    55→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    56→| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
    57→| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
    58→| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
    59→| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
    60→| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
    61→| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |
    62→
    63→### 2.4 Control Flow
    64→
    65→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    66→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    67→| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
    68→| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
    69→| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
    70→| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure (static link = 0) |
    71→| `calln` depth8 addr24 | 5 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call with static link (depth=0: nested, depth=N: N levels up) |
    72→| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
    73→| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
    74→| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |
    75→
    76→### 2.5 Local / Global / Nonlocal Access
    77→
    78→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    79→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    80→| `enter` nlocals8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Set up frame, reserve N local slots |
    81→| `leave` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Tear down frame |
    82→| `loadl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load local at offset |
    83→| `storel` off8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store local at offset |
    84→| `loadg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load global at offset |
    85→| `storeg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store global at offset |
    86→| `addrl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of local |
    87→| `addrg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of global |
    88→| `loadn` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load nonlocal via static link chain |
    89→| `storen` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store nonlocal via static link chain |
    90→
    91→### 2.6 Indirect Memory Access
    92→
    93→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    94→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    95→| `load` | 1 byte | ( addr -- val ) | Load word from address |
    96→| `store` | 1 byte | ( val addr -- ) | Store word to address |
    97→| `loadb` | 1 byte | ( addr -- byte ) | Load byte (zero-extended) |
    98→| `storeb` | 1 byte | ( byte addr -- ) | Store byte |
    99→
   100→### 2.7 System / I/O
   101→
   102→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
   103→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
   104→| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |
   105→
   106→System call IDs:
   107→
   108→| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
   109→|----|------|-------------|-------------|
   110→| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
   111→| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
   112→| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
   113→| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED state |
   114→| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
   115→| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |
   116→
   117→### 2.8 Argument Access
   118→
   119→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
   120→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
   121→| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
   122→| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |
   123→
   124→## 3. Call Frame Layout
   125→
   126→When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:
   127→
   128→```
   129→         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
   130→         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
   131→         │ ...              │
   132→         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
   133→fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
   134→         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
   135→         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
   136→         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
   137→         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
   138→         ├──────────────────┤
   139→         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
   140→         │ ...              │
   141→         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
   142→         └──────────────────┘
   143→```
   144→
   145→- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
   146→- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
   147→  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
   148→- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
   149→- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp
   150→- **ret N**: pushes return value (if any) to eval stack, pops frame, cleans N args
   151→
   152→## 4. Calling Convention
   153→
   154→Simple and explicit:
   155→
   156→1. Caller pushes arguments left-to-right onto eval stack
   157→2. Caller executes `call <proc_addr>`
   158→3. Callee executes `enter <nlocals>` (reserves local slots)
   159→4. Callee accesses args via `loada 0`, `loada 1`, etc.
   160→5. Callee accesses locals via `loadl 0`, `loadl 1`, etc.
   161→6. For functions: callee pushes return value onto eval stack
   162→7. Callee executes `ret <nargs>` (cleans args from frame)
   163→8. Caller finds return value (if any) on eval stack
   164→
   165→## 5. P-Code Assembler (pasm) Syntax
   166→
   167→### 5.0 File Extensions
   168→
   169→- `.spc` — p-code assembler source files (input to pasm)
   170→- `.p24` — assembled p-code bytecode files (output of pasm, input to VM)
   171→
   172→### 5.1 Source Format
   173→
   174→```
   175→; Comment (semicolon to end of line)
   176→
   177→.const UART_DATA  -65280      ; named constant
   178→.const UART_STATUS -65279
   179→.const LED_PORT   -65024
   180→
   181→.global counter 1             ; 1 word of global storage
   182→.global buffer 32             ; 32 words
   183→
   184→.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes
   185→
   186→.proc main 0                 ; procedure, 0 locals
   187→    push msg                  ; push address of msg
   188→    call puts
   189→    halt
   190→.end
   191→
   192→.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (loop pointer)
   193→    loada 0                   ; arg0 = string address
   194→    storel 0                  ; local0 = current ptr
   195→loop:
   196→    loadl 0
   197→    loadb                     ; load byte at ptr
   198→    dup
   199→    jz done                   ; if zero, done
   200→    sys 1                     ; PUTC
   201→    loadl 0
   202→    push 1
   203→    add
   204→    storel 0                  ; ptr++
   205→    jmp loop
   206→done:
   207→    drop                      ; drop the zero
   208→    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 arg
   209→.end
   210→```
   211→
   212→### 5.2 Directives
   213→
   214→| Directive | Syntax | Description |
   215→|-----------|--------|-------------|
   216→| `.const` | `.const NAME value` | Define named constant |
   217→| `.global` | `.global NAME nwords` | Reserve global storage |
   218→| `.data` | `.data NAME byte, byte, ...` | Define byte data (string/array) |
   219→| `.proc` | `.proc NAME nlocals` | Begin procedure (nlocals = local slot count) |
   220→| `.end` | `.end` | End procedure |
   221→
   222→### 5.3 Labels
   223→
   224→Labels appear on their own line, ending with `:`:
   225→
   226→```
   227→loop:
   228→    ; instructions...
   229→    jmp loop
   230→```
   231→
   232→Labels are local to the enclosing `.proc` / `.end` block.
   233→
   234→### 5.4 Integer Literals
   235→
   236→- Decimal: `42`, `-1`, `0`
   237→- Hex: `0xFF` (if supported) or decimal equivalents
   238→- Named constants: `UART_DATA` (substituted by assembler)
   239→
   240→### 5.5 Instructions
   241→
   242→One instruction per line. Operand follows mnemonic separated by whitespace:
   243→
   244→```
   245→push 42
   246→loadl 0
   247→jz done
   248→call puts
   249→sys 1
   250→ret 0
   251→```
   252→
   253→## 6. Opcode Encoding Table
   254→
   255→Opcodes are assigned sequential byte values:
   256→
   257→| Byte | Mnemonic | Operand |
   258→|------|----------|---------|
   259→| 0x00 | halt | — |
   260→| 0x01 | push | imm24 |
   261→| 0x02 | push_s | imm8 |
   262→| 0x03 | dup | — |
   263→| 0x04 | drop | — |
   264→| 0x05 | swap | — |
   265→| 0x06 | over | — |
   266→| 0x10 | add | — |
   267→| 0x11 | sub | — |
   268→| 0x12 | mul | — |
   269→| 0x13 | div | — |
   270→| 0x14 | mod | — |
   271→| 0x15 | neg | — |
   272→| 0x16 | and | — |
   273→| 0x17 | or | — |
   274→| 0x18 | xor | — |
   275→| 0x19 | not | — |
   276→| 0x1A | shl | — |
   277→| 0x1B | shr | — |
   278→| 0x20 | eq | — |
   279→| 0x21 | ne | — |
   280→| 0x22 | lt | — |
   281→| 0x23 | le | — |
   282→| 0x24 | gt | — |
   283→| 0x25 | ge | — |
   284→| 0x30 | jmp | addr24 |
   285→| 0x31 | jz | addr24 |
   286→| 0x32 | jnz | addr24 |
   287→| 0x33 | call | addr24 |
   288→| 0x34 | ret | nargs8 |
   289→| 0x35 | calln | depth8 addr24 |
   290→| 0x36 | trap | code8 |
   291→| 0x40 | enter | nlocals8 |
   292→| 0x41 | leave | — |
   293→| 0x42 | loadl | off8 |
   294→| 0x43 | storel | off8 |
   295→| 0x44 | loadg | off24 |
   296→| 0x45 | storeg | off24 |
   297→| 0x46 | addrl | off8 |
   298→| 0x47 | addrg | off24 |
   299→| 0x48 | loada | idx8 |
   300→| 0x49 | storea | idx8 |
   301→| 0x4A | loadn | depth8 off8 |
   302→| 0x4B | storen | depth8 off8 |
   303→| 0x50 | load | — |
   304→| 0x51 | store | — |
   305→| 0x52 | loadb | — |
   306→| 0x53 | storeb | — |
   307→| 0x60 | sys | id8 |
   308→
   309→Reserved ranges 0x70–0xFF for future opcodes (heap, debug, extended ops).
   310→
   311→## 7. Trap Codes
   312→
   313→| Code | Name | Trigger |
   314→|------|------|---------|
   315→| 0 | USER_TRAP | `trap 0` instruction |
   316→| 1 | DIV_ZERO | Division/modulo by zero |
   317→| 2 | STACK_OVERFLOW | Eval or call stack exceeds limit |
   318→| 3 | STACK_UNDERFLOW | Eval stack pop when empty |
   319→| 4 | INVALID_OPCODE | Unknown opcode byte |
   320→| 5 | INVALID_ADDRESS | Memory access out of bounds |
   321→| 6 | NIL_POINTER | Dereference of address 0 |
   322→| 7 | BOUNDS_CHECK | Array index out of bounds |
   323→
   324→On trap: VM sets status=2, trap_code=N, halts. Diagnostic message to UART.
   325→
   326→## 8. Memory Segment Layout
   327→
   328→At VM initialization:
   329→
   330→1. **Code segment** starts at a fixed base address (after VM code)
   331→2. **Constants/data** follows code (emitted by pasm)
   332→3. **Globals** follow data (allocated per `.global` directives)
   333→4. **Call stack** starts after globals, grows upward
   334→5. **Eval stack** starts after call stack region, grows upward
   335→6. **Heap** starts after eval stack region, grows upward
   336→7. Segment base addresses stored in VM state struct
   337→
   338→Sizes are configurable at VM init time. Default allocations:
   339→- Call stack: 4 KB
   340→- Eval stack: 2 KB
   341→- Heap: remaining SRAM up to ~900 KB
   342→
   343→## 9. Implementation Strategy
   344→
   345→### Phase 1: Minimal VM
   346→- Opcode dispatch loop
   347→- push, dup, drop, add, sub, halt
   348→- UART output via sys PUTC
   349→- Hardcoded test bytecode (no pasm yet)
   350→
   351→### Phase 2: Control Flow + Procedures
   352→- jmp, jz, jnz
   353→- call, ret, enter, leave
   354→- loadl, storel, loada, storea
   355→- Frame management
   356→
   357→### Phase 3: Full Instruction Set
   358→- All arithmetic/logic/comparison opcodes
   359→- Globals (loadg, storeg)
   360→- Indirect memory (load, store, loadb, storeb)
   361→- Nonlocal access (loadn, storen)
   362→- All sys calls
   363→
   364→### Phase 4: P-Code Assembler
   365→- Lexer for pasm syntax (UART input)
   366→- Two-pass assembly (collect labels, emit bytes)
   367→- Directive handling (.const, .global, .data, .proc/.end)
   368→- Output bytecode to memory buffer
   369→
   370→### Phase 5: Integration & Test Suite
   371→- pasm assembles test programs
   372→- VM executes assembled bytecode
   373→- Regression test harness (demo.sh)
   374→- Full test coverage per staged plan
   375→
   376→## 10. COR24 Assembly Patterns
   377→
   378→Reference patterns from tf24a for common idioms:
   379→
   380→### Dispatch Table
   381→```asm
   382→; Index into jump table by opcode byte
   383→; r0 = opcode
   384→la r2, dispatch_table
   385→; multiply r0 by 3 (entry size = .word = 3 bytes)
   386→; ... load target address, jmp
   387→```
   388→
   389→### UART Output
   390→```asm
   391→; r0 = character to send
   392→la r2, -65280       ; UART base
   393→tx_wait:
   394→    lb r0, 1(r2)   ; status (sign-extended)
   395→    cls r0, z       ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
   396→    brt tx_wait
   397→    sb r0, 0(r2)   ; write byte
   398→```
   399→
   400→### Memory Struct Access
   401→```asm
   402→; Load vm_state.pc into r0
   403→la fp, vm_state
   404→lw r0, 0(fp)       ; pc at offset 0
   405→```

1→; pasm.s — P-Code Assembler for COR24
     2→;
     3→; Two-pass assembler: reads .spc source from UART, assembles to bytecode.
     4→;   Pass 1: collect symbols (labels, procs, consts, globals, data), compute sizes
     5→;   Pass 2: emit bytecode, resolve symbol references
     6→;
     7→; Token types:
     8→;   0 = TOK_EOF     end of input
     9→;   1 = TOK_NL      newline
    10→;   2 = TOK_NUM     integer literal (value in tok_value)
    11→;   3 = TOK_IDENT   identifier/mnemonic/name (string in tok_buf)
    12→;   4 = TOK_DIR     directive without '.' (string in tok_buf)
    13→;   5 = TOK_LABEL   label definition without ':' (string in tok_buf)
    14→;   6 = TOK_COMMA   comma separator
    15→;
    16→; Symbol types:
    17→;   0 = SYM_CONST   named constant (value = literal)
    18→;   1 = SYM_LABEL   code label (value = code offset)
    19→;   2 = SYM_PROC    procedure entry (value = code offset)
    20→;   3 = SYM_GLOBAL  global variable (value = global segment offset)
    21→;   4 = SYM_DATA    data block (value = data segment offset)
    22→;
    23→; Operand types (mnemonic table):
    24→;   0 = NONE    1-byte instruction
    25→;   1 = IMM8    2-byte: opcode + byte
    26→;   2 = IMM24   4-byte: opcode + word
    27→;   3 = D8_A24  5-byte: opcode + byte + word (calln)
    28→;   4 = D8_O8   3-byte: opcode + byte + byte (loadn/storen)
    29→;
    30→; Register allocation:
    31→;   r0 = work/scratch, parameter, return value
    32→;   r1 = return address (jal) or scratch
    33→;   r2 = scratch, function address for jal
    34→;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
    35→;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR)
    36→;
    37→; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
    38→;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
    39→;   RX ready = status bit 0
    40→
    41→; ============================================================
    42→; Entry point
    43→; ============================================================
    44→_start:
    45→    ; Print boot message
    46→    la r0, msg_boot
    47→    la r2, uart_puts
    48→    jal r1, (r2)
    49→
    50→    ; Read all UART input into input_buf
    51→    la r2, read_all_input
    52→    jal r1, (r2)
    53→
    54→    ; ---- Pass 1: collect symbols, compute sizes ----
    55→    lc r0, 0
    56→    la r2, input_pos
    57→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    58→    ; Prime first character
    59→    la r2, lex_advance
    60→    jal r1, (r2)
    61→    ; Set pass number = 1
    62→    lc r0, 1
    63→    la r2, pass_num
    64→    sb r0, 0(r2)
    65→    ; Reset counters
    66→    lc r0, 0
    67→    la r2, code_addr
    68→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    69→    la r2, global_offset
    70→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    71→    la r2, data_offset
    72→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    73→    la r2, sym_count
    74→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    75→    ; Initialize name_pool_ptr
    76→    la r0, name_pool
    77→    la r2, name_pool_ptr
    78→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    79→    ; Run pass 1
    80→    la r2, parse_program
    81→    jal r1, (r2)
    82→
    83→    ; Save code size after pass 1
    84→    la r2, code_addr
    85→    lw r0, 0(r2)
    86→    la r2, code_size
    87→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    88→    ; Save data size
    89→    la r2, data_offset
    90→    lw r0, 0(r2)
    91→    la r2, total_data_size
    92→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    93→
    94→    ; Patch data and global symbols with absolute offsets
    95→    la r2, patch_symbols

    98→    ; ---- Pass 2: emit bytecode ----
    99→    lc r0, 0
   100→    la r2, input_pos
   101→    sw r0, 0(r2)
   102→    ; Prime first character
   103→    la r2, lex_advance
   104→    jal r1, (r2)
   105→    ; Set pass number = 2
   106→    lc r0, 2
   107→    la r2, pass_num
   108→    sb r0, 0(r2)
   109→    ; Initialize code output pointer
   110→    la r0, code_buf
   111→    la r2, code_ptr

   114→    la r0, data_buf

   122→    la r2, dump_bytecode

   124→
   125→    ; Done
   126→    la r0, msg_done
   127→    la r2, uart_puts
   128→    jal r1, (r2)
   129→halt_loop:

   132→; ============================================================
   133→; UART helpers
   134→; ============================================================
   135→
   136→; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
   137→; Leaf function. Clobbers: r0, r2. Preserves: r1.
   138→uart_putc:
   139→    push r0

   141→uart_putc_wait:
   142→    lb r0, 1(r2)
   143→    cls r0, z
   144→    brt uart_putc_wait
   145→    pop r0
   146→    sb r0, 0(r2)
   147→    jmp (r1)
   148→

1→; pvm.s — P-Code Virtual Machine for COR24
     2→;
     3→; Register allocation:
     4→;   r0 = W (work/scratch, opcode dispatch)
     5→;   r1 = scratch / return address for jal calls
     6→;   r2 = scratch
     7→;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR, used sparingly)
     8→;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
     9→;
    10→; VM state lives in memory struct (vm_state) because COR24 has only 3 GPRs.
    11→;
    12→; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
    13→;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
    14→;   RX ready = status bit 0
    15→; LED: port at -65024 (0xFF0200)
    16→;
    17→; COR24 ISA notes:
    18→;   lbu = load byte zero-extend, lb = load byte sign-extend
    19→;   ceq ra, rb sets C if ra == rb; cls ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (signed)
    20→;   clu ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (unsigned)
    21→;   brt/brf = branch if C true/false; bra = branch always
    22→;   jal r1, (r2) = r1 = PC+1, PC = r2 (call convention)
    23→;   jmp (r1) = return from jal call
    24→;   Valid load/store base registers: r0, r1, r2, fp (NOT sp)
    25→;   Can push/pop: r0, r1, r2, fp
    26→
    27→; ============================================================
    28→; Entry point
    29→; ============================================================
    30→_start:
    31→    ; Initialize VM state struct (fp = &vm_state)
    32→    la r0, vm_state
    33→    push r0
    34→    pop fp
    35→
    36→    ; pc = 0
    37→    lc r0, 0
    38→    sw r0, 0(fp)
    39→
    40→    ; esp = eval_stack base
    41→    la r0, eval_stack
    42→    sw r0, 3(fp)
    43→
    44→    ; csp = call_stack base
    45→    la r0, call_stack
    46→    sw r0, 6(fp)
    47→
    48→    ; fp_vm = 0 (no frame yet)
    49→    lc r0, 0
    50→    sw r0, 9(fp)
    51→
    52→    ; gp = globals base
    53→    la r0, globals_seg
    54→    sw r0, 12(fp)
    55→
    56→    ; hp = heap base
    57→    la r0, heap_seg
    58→    sw r0, 15(fp)
    59→
    60→    ; code = code segment base
    61→    la r0, code_seg
    62→    sw r0, 18(fp)
    63→
    64→    ; status = 0 (running)
    65→    lc r0, 0
    66→    sw r0, 21(fp)
    67→
    68→    ; trap_code = 0
    69→    lc r0, 0
    70→    sw r0, 24(fp)
    71→
    72→    ; Print boot message
    73→    la r0, msg_boot
    74→    la r2, uart_puts
    75→    jal r1, (r2)
    76→
    77→    ; Enter VM main loop
    78→    la r0, vm_loop
    79→    jmp (r0)
    80→
    81→; ============================================================
    82→; UART helpers
    83→; ============================================================
    84→
    85→; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
    86→; Call via: jal r1, (r2) with r2 = uart_putc
    87→; Clobbers: r0, r2 (r1 = return address, preserved)
    88→uart_putc:
    89→    push r0
    90→    la r2, -65280
    91→uart_putc_wait:
    92→    lb r0, 1(r2)
    93→    cls r0, z
    94→    brt uart_putc_wait
    95→    pop r0
    96→    sb r0, 0(r2)
    97→    jmp (r1)
    98→
    99→; uart_puts — print null-terminated string at address in r0
   100→; Call via: jal r1, (r2) with r2 = uart_puts, r0 = string addr
   101→; Clobbers: r0, r1, r2
   102→uart_puts:
   103→    push r1
   104→    mov r1, r0
   105→uart_puts_loop:
   106→    lbu r0, 0(r1)
   107→    ceq r0, z
   108→    brt uart_puts_done
   109→    push r1
   110→    push r0
   111→    la r2, -65280
   112→uart_puts_tx:
   113→    lb r0, 1(r2)
   114→    cls r0, z
   115→    brt uart_puts_tx
   116→    pop r0
   117→    sb r0, 0(r2)
   118→    pop r1
   119→    add r1, 1
   120→    bra uart_puts_loop
   121→uart_puts_done:
   122→    pop r1
   123→    jmp (r1)
   124→
   125→; ============================================================
   126→; VM fetch-decode-execute loop
   127→; ============================================================
   128→vm_loop:
   129→    ; Check status: if not running (0), stop
   130→    la r0, vm_state
   131→    push r0
   132→    pop fp
   133→    lw r0, 21(fp)
   134→    ceq r0, z
   135→    brf vm_halted
   136→
   137→    ; Fetch opcode byte from code[pc]
   138→    lw r0, 18(fp)
   139→    lw r2, 0(fp)
   140→    add r0, r2
   141→    ; r0 = code_base + pc, use r0 as base register
   142→    lbu r2, 0(r0)
   143→    ; r2 = opcode byte (zero-extended)
   144→
   145→    ; Increment pc
   146→    lw r0, 0(fp)
   147→    add r0, 1
   148→    sw r0, 0(fp)
   149→
   150→    ; Bounds check: opcode must be < 97 (0x00..0x60)

175→; read_all_input — read UART into input_buf until EOT (0x04)
   176→; ============================================================
   177→; Non-leaf. Clobbers: r0, r1, r2.
   178→read_all_input:
   179→    push r1
   180→    ; Initialize write pointer and length
   181→    la r0, input_buf
   182→    la r2, rai_ptr
   183→    sw r0, 0(r2)
   184→    lc r0, 0
   185→    la r2, input_len
   186→    sw r0, 0(r2)
   187→
   188→rai_loop:
   189→    ; Poll UART RX ready
   190→    la r2, -65280
   191→rai_poll:
   192→    lbu r0, 1(r2)
   193→    push r2
   194→    lc r2, 1
   195→    and r0, r2
   196→    pop r2
   197→    ceq r0, z
   198→    brt rai_poll
   199→    ; Read byte
   200→    lbu r0, 0(r2)
   201→    ; Check for EOT (0x04)
   202→    lc r2, 4
   203→    ceq r0, r2
   204→    brf rai_not_eot
   205→    la r0, rai_done
   206→    jmp (r0)
   207→rai_not_eot:
   208→    ; Store byte in buffer
   209→    la r2, rai_ptr
   210→    lw r2, 0(r2)
   211→    sb r0, 0(r2)
   212→    ; Advance pointer
   213→    la r2, rai_ptr
   214→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   215→    add r0, 1
   216→    sw r0, 0(r2)
   217→    ; Increment length
   218→    la r2, input_len
   219→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   220→    add r0, 1
   221→    sw r0, 0(r2)
   222→    bra rai_loop
   223→
   224→rai_done:
   225→    ; Null-terminate buffer
   226→    la r2, rai_ptr
   227→    lw r2, 0(r2)
   228→    lc r0, 0
   229→    sb r0, 0(r2)
   230→    pop r1
   231→    jmp (r1)
   232→
   233→; ============================================================
   234→; Lexer — character input (buffer-based)
   235→; ============================================================
   236→
   237→; lex_advance — read next character from input_buf
   238→; If past end, sets lex_char = 0 (EOF).
   239→; Leaf function. Clobbers: r0, r2. Preserves: r1.
   240→lex_advance:
   241→    la r2, input_pos
   242→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   243→    ; Save position for comparison
   244→    push r0
   245→    la r2, input_len
   246→    lw r2, 0(r2)
   247→    pop r0
   248→    clu r0, r2
   249→    brf lex_adv_eof
   250→    ; Read byte from input_buf[position]
   251→    la r2, input_buf
   252→    add r2, r0
   253→    lbu r0, 0(r2)
   254→    ; Advance position

   730→    ; IDENT (3) → handle instruction
   731→    lc r2, 3
   732→    ceq r0, r2
   733→    brf pp_skip
   734→    la r2, handle_instr
   735→    jal r1, (r2)
   736→    la r0, pp_loop
   737→    jmp (r0)
   738→pp_skip:

   751→    push r1
   752→
   753→    ; Check "const"
   754→    la r0, tok_buf
   755→    la r2, str_eq_a
   756→    sw r0, 0(r2)
   757→    la r0, dir_const_str
   758→    la r2, str_eq_b
   759→    sw r0, 0(r2)
   760→    la r2, str_eq
   761→    jal r1, (r2)
   762→    ceq r0, z

   765→    jal r1, (r2)
   766→    pop r1
   767→    jmp (r1)
   768→hd_not_const:

   770→    ; Check "global"
   771→    la r0, tok_buf
   772→    la r2, str_eq_a

    85→; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART

    88→uart_putc:
    89→    push r0

    91→uart_putc_wait:

18:;   lbu = load byte zero-extend, lb = load byte sign-extend
82:; UART helpers

1527:; 0x52 — loadb: ( addr -- byte ) load byte zero-extended
1666:; sys PUTC (id=1): pop char from eval stack, send to UART
1686:; sys GETC (id=2): read byte from UART, push onto eval stack
1688:    ; Poll UART status until RX ready (bit 0)
2009:; Expected UART output: PVM OK\nOK\nTRAP 0\n

1686→; sys GETC (id=2): read byte from UART, push onto eval stack
  1687→sys_getc:
  1688→    ; Poll UART status until RX ready (bit 0)
  1689→sys_getc_wait:
  1690→    la r2, -65280
  1691→    lbu r0, 1(r2)
  1692→    ; bit 0 = RX ready; mask it
  1693→    lc r2, 1
  1694→    and r0, r2
  1695→    ceq r0, z
  1696→    brt sys_getc_wait
  1697→    ; RX ready — read data byte
  1698→    la r2, -65280
  1699→    lbu r0, 0(r2)
  1700→    ; r0 = received byte; push onto eval stack
  1701→    la r2, vm_state
  1702→    push r2
  1703→    pop fp
  1704→    lw r2, 3(fp)
  1705→    ; r2 = esp
  1706→    sw r0, 0(r2)
  1707→    add r2, 3
  1708→    sw r2, 3(fp)
  1709→    la r0, vm_loop
  1710→    jmp (r0)
  1711→
  1712→; sys LED (id=3): pop state from eval stack, write to LED port
  1713→sys_led:
  1714→    ; fp = &vm_state
  1715→    lw r2, 3(fp)
  1716→    add r2, -3
  1717→    sw r2, 3(fp)
  1718→    ; r2 = new esp = &TOS
  1719→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1720→    ; r0 = LED state
  1721→    la r2, -65024
  1722→    sb r0, 0(r2)
  1723→    la r0, vm_loop
  1724→    jmp (r0)
  1725→
  1726→; sys ALLOC (id=4): pop size, bump-allocate, push pointer
  1727→; Uses nonlocal_temps as scratch: [0]=size, [3]=old hp (return ptr)
  1728→sys_alloc:
  1729→    la r0, vm_state
  1730→    push r0
  1731→    pop fp
  1732→    ; Pop size from eval stack
  1733→    lw r2, 3(fp)
  1734→    add r2, -3
  1735→    sw r2, 3(fp)
  1736→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1737→    ; r0 = size
  1738→    ; Save size to nonlocal_temps[0]
  1739→    la r2, nonlocal_temps
  1740→    push r2
  1741→    pop fp
  1742→    sw r0, 0(fp)
  1743→    ; Load current hp and save as return pointer to nonlocal_temps[3]
  1744→    la r0, vm_state
  1745→    push r0

1→# pv24a -- P-Code VM for COR24
     2→
     3→A p-code virtual machine and p-code assembler (pasm), both written in COR24 assembly. pv24a provides a language-neutral stack-based execution substrate for compilers (Pascal, Lisp, BASIC, etc.) that emit p-code bytecode.
     4→
     5→Runs on the COR24 emulator ([cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs)) and eventually on FPGA hardware ([COR24-TB](https://makerlisp.com)).
     6→
     7→## Project Status
     8→
     9→The VM interpreter is **functional** -- 30+ opcodes implemented with trap handling, all verified with embedded test bytecode. The p-code assembler (pasm) lexer is complete.
    10→
    11→### What Works
    12→
    13→- Stack operations: push, push_s, dup, drop, swap, over
    14→- Arithmetic: add, sub, mul, div, mod, neg
    15→- Bitwise: and, or, xor, not, shl, shr
    16→- Comparison: eq, ne, lt, le, gt, ge (push 1/0)
    17→- Control flow: jmp, jz, jnz
    18→- Procedures: call, calln, ret, enter, leave
    19→- Local/argument access: loadl, storel, loada, storea
    20→- Global access: loadg, storeg, addrg, addrl
    21→- Nonlocal access: loadn, storen (static link chain traversal for nested Pascal procedures)
    22→- Indirect memory: load, store, loadb, storeb
    23→- System calls: HALT, PUTC, GETC, LED, ALLOC (bump allocator), FREE
    24→
    25→### What's Next
    26→
    27→- P-code assembler (pasm) -- parser, two-pass assembly (lexer done)
    28→- Test suite with golden output comparison
    29→- End-to-end: pasm source -> bytecode -> VM execution
    30→
    31→## Related
    32→
    33→- [tf24a](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/tf24a) -- DTC Forth in COR24 assembly
    34→- [tc24r](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/tc24r) -- Tiny C compiler for COR24 (Rust)
    35→- [tml24c](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/tml24c) -- Tiny Macro Lisp for COR24
    36→- [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) -- COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)
    37→- [COR24-TB](https://makerlisp.com) -- The COR24 FPGA target board
    38→
    39→## Documentation
    40→
    41→| Document | Description |
    42→|----------|-------------|
    43→| [Design](docs/design.md) | VM spec: opcodes, encoding, call frame layout, pasm syntax |
    44→| [Architecture](docs/architecture.md) | System layers, memory map, register allocation |
    45→| [PRD](docs/prd.md) | Product requirements, deliverables, success criteria |
    46→| [Research](docs/research.txt) | Deep research on p-code VM design and implementation approach |
    47→
    48→## Building and Running
    49→
    50→Requires [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) (`cor24-run` binary).
    51→
    52→```bash
    53→# Assemble and run the VM with embedded test bytecode
    54→cor24-run --run pvm.s --speed 0
    55→
    56→# With UART input (for GETC testing)
    57→cor24-run --run pvm.s -u 'A' --speed 0 -n 5000000
    58→
    59→# Dump CPU state after halt
    60→cor24-run --run pvm.s --dump --speed 0
    61→
    62→# Trace last N instructions
    63→cor24-run --run pvm.s --trace 100 --speed 0
    64→
    65→# Run pasm lexer with source from UART (EOT = end of input)
    66→cor24-run --run pasm.s -u "$(printf '.proc main 0\npush 42\nhalt\n.end\n\x04')" --speed 0 -n 5000000
    67→```
    68→
    69→## COR24 Architecture at a Glance
    70→
    71→- **24-bit** registers and address space (16 MB)
    72→- **3 GPRs**: r0, r1, r2 (plus fp, sp, z)
    73→- **Variable-length** instruction encoding (1/2/4 bytes)
    74→- Hardware multiply, no hardware divide
    75→- Little-endian, byte-addressable
    76→- UART and LED via memory-mapped I/O
    77→
    78→## License
    79→
    80→MIT -- see [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details.
    81→
    82→Copyright (c) 2026 Michael A Wright
    83→

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me create one final comprehensive summary document:

## Comprehensive Exploration of pv24a Project

I've thoroughly analyzed the pv24a P-Code VM project. Here's what I found:

---

## 1. PASM.S - P-Code Assembler

**How it works:**
- Two-pass assembler that reads p-code source from UART and assembles to bytecode
- Reads source via `read_all_input()` which polls UART RX (0xFF0101 bit 0) until EOT (0x04 marker)
- Stores input in 512-byte `input_buf` buffer

**Memory Layout (pasm buffers):**
- `input_buf` - 512 bytes: reads source code from UART
- `input_pos` - tracks current read position in input
- `sym_table` - 288 bytes (32 symbols × 9 bytes each): stores consts, labels, procs, globals, data
- `name_pool` - 256 bytes: stores symbol name strings
- `code_buf` - 256 bytes: output p-code bytecode during Pass 2
- `code_ptr` - pointer to next write location in code_buf
- `data_buf` - 128 bytes: output data/constants during Pass 2
- `data_ptr` - pointer to next write location in data_buf

**Pass 1 (lines 54-96):**
- Resets counters: `code_addr`, `global_offset`, `data_offset`, `sym_count`
- Lexes and parses entire program, collecting symbols
- Calculates final sizes and stores them in `code_size`, `total_data_size`
- Calls `patch_symbols()` to convert symbol values to absolute offsets

**Pass 2 (lines 98-123):**
- Resets `code_ptr` to `code_buf`, `data_ptr` to `data_buf`
- Re-lexes entire program, emits bytes to code_buf and data_buf via `emit_byte()` and `emit_word()`
- After pass 2 completes, calls `dump_bytecode()` to verify output

**Output/Bytecode Handling:**
- `dump_bytecode()` (line 1838): Prints assembled bytes to UART as decimal values (for verification)
- Bytecode is stored in `code_buf` and `data_buf` (local SRAM buffers within pasm.s)
- **No automatic transfer to VM** - after pasm finishes, it halts at line 129-130

**Key I/O Points:**
- **Input**: Reads from UART (0xFF0100 data, 0xFF0101 status) until EOT marker
- **Output**: Writes verification dump to UART (via `uart_putc`)
- **Bytecode storage**: In local `code_buf` and `data_buf` (256 + 128 = 384 bytes max)

---

## 2. PVM.S - P-Code Virtual Machine

**VM Initialization (lines 30-79):**
- Initializes `vm_state` struct with pointers to memory segments
- Sets initial state: pc=0, status=0 (running), trap_code=0
- Sets up stack and segment pointers

**VM State Structure** (line 1984):
```
Offset 0:   pc         - program counter (word)
Offset 3:   esp        - eval stack pointer (word)
Offset 6:   csp        - call stack pointer (word)
Offset 9:   fp_vm      - frame pointer (word)
Offset 12:  gp         - globals base address (word)
Offset 15:  hp         - heap pointer (word)
Offset 18:  code       - code segment base address (word)
Offset 21:  status     - 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped (word)
Offset 24:  trap_code  - trap reason (word)
Total: 27 bytes (9 words × 3 bytes each)
```

**Bytecode Reading:**
- VM reads bytecode from `code_seg` (hardcoded at line 2028)
- Fetch loop (lines 138-142): `code_base + pc` calculation
  - Loads from `vm_state.code` (pointer to code segment)
  - Adds current `pc` offset
  - Uses as address to read next opcode byte via `lbu`

**Memory Layout (pvm.s segments):**
```
code_seg          - lines 2028-2039    (hardcoded test bytecode, 11 bytes)
globals_seg       - lines 2042-2050    (8 words = 24 bytes)
call_stack        - lines 2053-2086    (32 words = 96 bytes, grows upward)
eval_stack        - lines 2088-2121    (32 words = 96 bytes, grows upward)
heap_seg          - lines 2123-2156    (32 words = 96 bytes, grows upward)
```

**Current Test Bytecode** (hardcoded):
```
Offset 0:  2, 79      - push_s 79 ('O')
Offset 2:  96, 1      - sys 1 (PUTC)
Offset 4:  2, 75      - push_s 75 ('K')
Offset 6:  96, 1      - sys 1 (PUTC)
Offset 8:  2, 10      - push_s 10 ('\n')
Offset 10: 96, 1      - sys 1 (PUTC)
Offset 12: 54, 0      - trap 0 (USER_TRAP)
Offset 14: 96, 0      - sys 0 (HALT)
```

**Execution Loop** (lines 128-150):
1. Check status (must be 0 to continue)
2. Fetch opcode from `code_base + pc`
3. Increment pc
4. Dispatch to opcode handler
5. Handler modifies vm_state and returns to vm_loop

**I/O Points:**
- **Bytecode source**: Hard-wired to `code_seg` in pvm.s (static, not from UART)
- **UART output**: Via `sys 1` (PUTC) - writes to 0xFF0100
- **UART input**: Via `sys 2` (GETC) - polls 0xFF0100 status bit 0, reads from 0xFF0100
- **LED control**: Via `sys 3` - writes to 0xFF0200

---

## 3. Memory Map (Complete COR24 Address Space)

From `docs/architecture.md` (lines 62-99):
```
0x000000  ┌──────────────────┐
          │ COR24 boot/entry │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ P-Code VM code   │  (pvm.s assembled as COR24 instructions)
          │ (interpreter)    │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ P-Code bytecode  │  (code_seg in pvm.s or output from pasm)
          │ (code segment)   │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Constants/data   │  (literal pool)
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Globals segment  │  (p-code global variables)
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Call stack       │  (frames, grows upward)
          │ (grows upward)   │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Eval stack       │  (expression temps, grows upward)
          │ (grows upward)   │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Free space       │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Heap             │  (dynamic allocation, grows upward)
          │ (bump allocator) │
          ├──────────────────┤
0x0FFFFF  │ End of SRAM      │
          ├──────────────────┤
0xFEEC00  │ EBR (3KB)        │  COR24 hardware stack (sp)
0xFEF7FF  └──────────────────┘
0xFF0100    UART data register
0xFF0101    UART status register
0xFF0200    LED port
```

---

## 4. How They Connect (Current Limitation)

**Current State:**
- pasm.s reads from UART, assembles to local buffers (`code_buf`, `data_buf`)
- pvm.s has hard-wired bytecode in `code_seg`
- **They are NOT currently connected** - pasm output doesn't flow to pvm

**To Connect Them End-to-End:**
Need to:
1. Have pasm write bytecode to a shared memory location after Pass 2
2. Have pvm read from that location instead of hard-wired code_seg
3. Or have pasm invoke pvm after assembly completes

**Key Integration Point:**
- pasm `code_buf` (256 bytes) could be relocated to match where pvm expects `code_seg`
- Or pvm could be modified to read from a configurable code base address
- Pass 2 in pasm completes at line 119, then dumps output at line 122-123

---

## 5. Symbol Table & Assembler Data Structures

**Symbol Entry Layout** (9 bytes each):
```
Byte 0:     sym_type    (0=const, 1=label, 2=proc, 3=global, 4=data)
Bytes 1-3:  sym_value   (24-bit: offset or immediate)
Bytes 4-8:  sym_name    (5-byte string ref or pointer, null-terminated in name_pool)
```

**Token Types:**
- 0 = TOK_EOF
- 1 = TOK_NL (newline)
- 2 = TOK_NUM (integer)
- 3 = TOK_IDENT (identifier/mnemonic)

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me create one final comprehensive summary document:

## Comprehensive Exploration of pv24a Project

I've thoroughly analyzed the pv24a P-Code VM project. Here's what I found:

---

## 1. PASM.S - P-Code Assembler

**How it works:**
- Two-pass assembler that reads p-code source from UART and assembles to bytecode
- Reads source via `read_all_input()` which polls UART RX (0xFF0101 bit 0) until EOT (0x04 marker)
- Stores input in 512-byte `input_buf` buffer

**Memory Layout (pasm buffers):**
- `input_buf` - 512 bytes: reads source code from UART
- `input_pos` - tracks current read position in input
- `sym_table` - 288 bytes (32 symbols × 9 bytes each): stores consts, labels, procs, globals, data
- `name_pool` - 256 bytes: stores symbol name strings
- `code_buf` - 256 bytes: output p-code bytecode during Pass 2
- `code_ptr` - pointer to next write location in code_buf
- `data_buf` - 128 bytes: output data/constants during Pass 2
- `data_ptr` - pointer to next write location in data_buf

**Pass 1 (lines 54-96):**
- Resets counters: `code_addr`, `global_offset`, `data_offset`, `sym_count`
- Lexes and parses entire program, collecting symbols
- Calculates final sizes and stores them in `code_size`, `total_data_size`
- Calls `patch_symbols()` to convert symbol values to absolute offsets

**Pass 2 (lines 98-123):**
- Resets `code_ptr` to `code_buf`, `data_ptr` to `data_buf`
- Re-lexes entire program, emits bytes to code_buf and data_buf via `emit_byte()` and `emit_word()`
- After pass 2 completes, calls `dump_bytecode()` to verify output

**Output/Bytecode Handling:**
- `dump_bytecode()` (line 1838): Prints assembled bytes to UART as decimal values (for verification)
- Bytecode is stored in `code_buf` and `data_buf` (local SRAM buffers within pasm.s)
- **No automatic transfer to VM** - after pasm finishes, it halts at line 129-130

**Key I/O Points:**
- **Input**: Reads from UART (0xFF0100 data, 0xFF0101 status) until EOT marker
- **Output**: Writes verification dump to UART (via `uart_putc`)
- **Bytecode storage**: In local `code_buf` and `data_buf` (256 + 128 = 384 bytes max)

---

## 2. PVM.S - P-Code Virtual Machine

**VM Initialization (lines 30-79):**
- Initializes `vm_state` struct with pointers to memory segments
- Sets initial state: pc=0, status=0 (running), trap_code=0
- Sets up stack and segment pointers

**VM State Structure** (line 1984):
```
Offset 0:   pc         - program counter (word)
Offset 3:   esp        - eval stack pointer (word)
Offset 6:   csp        - call stack pointer (word)
Offset 9:   fp_vm      - frame pointer (word)
Offset 12:  gp         - globals base address (word)
Offset 15:  hp         - heap pointer (word)
Offset 18:  code       - code segment base address (word)
Offset 21:  status     - 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped (word)
Offset 24:  trap_code  - trap reason (word)
Total: 27 bytes (9 words × 3 bytes each)
```

**Bytecode Reading:**
- VM reads bytecode from `code_seg` (hardcoded at line 2028)
- Fetch loop (lines 138-142): `code_base + pc` calculation
  - Loads from `vm_state.code` (pointer to code segment)
  - Adds current `pc` offset
  - Uses as address to read next opcode byte via `lbu`

**Memory Layout (pvm.s segments):**
```
code_seg          - lines 2028-2039    (hardcoded test bytecode, 11 bytes)
globals_seg       - lines 2042-2050    (8 words = 24 bytes)
call_stack        - lines 2053-2086    (32 words = 96 bytes, grows upward)
eval_stack        - lines 2088-2121    (32 words = 96 bytes, grows upward)
heap_seg          - lines 2123-2156    (32 words = 96 bytes, grows upward)
```

**Current Test Bytecode** (hardcoded):
```
Offset 0:  2, 79      - push_s 79 ('O')
Offset 2:  96, 1      - sys 1 (PUTC)
Offset 4:  2, 75      - push_s 75 ('K')
Offset 6:  96, 1      - sys 1 (PUTC)
Offset 8:  2, 10      - push_s 10 ('\n')
Offset 10: 96, 1      - sys 1 (PUTC)
Offset 12: 54, 0      - trap 0 (USER_TRAP)
Offset 14: 96, 0      - sys 0 (HALT)
```

**Execution Loop** (lines 128-150):
1. Check status (must be 0 to continue)
2. Fetch opcode from `code_base + pc`
3. Increment pc
4. Dispatch to opcode handler
5. Handler modifies vm_state and returns to vm_loop

**I/O Points:**
- **Bytecode source**: Hard-wired to `code_seg` in pvm.s (static, not from UART)
- **UART output**: Via `sys 1` (PUTC) - writes to 0xFF0100
- **UART input**: Via `sys 2` (GETC) - polls 0xFF0100 status bit 0, reads from 0xFF0100
- **LED control**: Via `sys 3` - writes to 0xFF0200

---

## 3. Memory Map (Complete COR24 Address Space)

From `docs/architecture.md` (lines 62-99):
```
0x000000  ┌──────────────────┐
          │ COR24 boot/entry │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ P-Code VM code   │  (pvm.s assembled as COR24 instructions)
          │ (interpreter)    │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ P-Code bytecode  │  (code_seg in pvm.s or output from pasm)
          │ (code segment)   │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Constants/data   │  (literal pool)
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Globals segment  │  (p-code global variables)
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Call stack       │  (frames, grows upward)
          │ (grows upward)   │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Eval stack       │  (expression temps, grows upward)
          │ (grows upward)   │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Free space       │
          ├──────────────────┤
          │ Heap             │  (dynamic allocation, grows upward)
          │ (bump allocator) │
          ├──────────────────┤
0x0FFFFF  │ End of SRAM      │
          ├──────────────────┤
0xFEEC00  │ EBR (3KB)        │  COR24 hardware stack (sp)
0xFEF7FF  └──────────────────┘
0xFF0100    UART data register
0xFF0101    UART status register
0xFF0200    LED port
```

---

## 4. How They Connect (Current Limitation)

**Current State:**
- pasm.s reads from UART, assembles to local buffers (`code_buf`, `data_buf`)
- pvm.s has hard-wired bytecode in `code_seg`
- **They are NOT currently connected** - pasm output doesn't flow to pvm

**To Connect Them End-to-End:**
Need to:
1. Have pasm write bytecode to a shared memory location after Pass 2
2. Have pvm read from that location instead of hard-wired code_seg
3. Or have pasm invoke pvm after assembly completes

**Key Integration Point:**
- pasm `code_buf` (256 bytes) could be relocated to match where pvm expects `code_seg`
- Or pvm could be modified to read from a configurable code base address
- Pass 2 in pasm completes at line 119, then dumps output at line 122-123

---

## 5. Symbol Table & Assembler Data Structures

**Symbol Entry Layout** (9 bytes each):
```
Byte 0:     sym_type    (0=const, 1=label, 2=proc, 3=global, 4=data)
Bytes 1-3:  sym_value   (24-bit: offset or immediate)
Bytes 4-8:  sym_name    (5-byte string ref or pointer, null-terminated in name_pool)
```

**Token Types:**
- 0 = TOK_EOF
- 1 = TOK_NL (newline)
- 2 = TOK_NUM (integer)
- 3 = TOK_IDENT (identifier/mnemonic)
- 4 = TOK_DIR (directive)
- 5 = TOK_LABEL (label definition)
- 6 = TOK_COMMA

**Operand Types (mnem_table):**
- 0 = NONE (1-byte instruction)
- 1 = IMM8 (2-byte: opcode + byte)
- 2 = IMM24 (4-byte: opcode + word)
- 3 = D8_A24 (5-byte: calln depth8 addr24)
- 4 = D8_O8 (3-byte: loadn/storen depth8 offset8)

---

## 6. .spc Files in Project

No `.spc` (p-code assembler source) files exist in the project yet. The project includes:
- `pasm.s` - assembler implementation (2543 lines COR24 asm)
- `pvm.s` - VM implementation (2155 lines COR24 asm)
- `docs/` - architecture, design, PRD documentation
- `.agentrail/` - development history and prompts

The syntax for `.spc` files is defined in `docs/design.md` (lines 165-251) but no example files exist.

---

## 7. Key Findings for End-to-End Integration

**pasm Output After Pass 2:**
- Bytecode stored in `code_buf` (starting at line 2521)
- Metadata in `code_ptr` tells how many bytes were emitted
- `dump_bytecode()` prints verification output to UART
- After dump, program halts (no continuation to VM)

**pvm Input Mechanism:**
- Currently hard-wired: `la r0, code_seg` (line 2028)
- Bytecode fetched via: `add r0, pc` then `lbu` from that address
- Could be redirected to read from any memory location

**Critical Bridge Needed:**
- Memory address that pasm writes to must match where pvm reads from
- pasm's `code_buf` (at whatever address it gets assembled to) must be accessible as `vm_state.code` base

file.content:
; pasm.s — P-Code Assembler for COR24
;
; Two-pass assembler: reads .spc source from UART, assembles to bytecode.
;   Pass 1: collect symbols (labels, procs, consts, globals, data), compute sizes
;   Pass 2: emit bytecode, resolve symbol references
;
; Token types:
;   0 = TOK_EOF     end of input
;   1 = TOK_NL      newline
;   2 = TOK_NUM     integer literal (value in tok_value)
;   3 = TOK_IDENT   identifier/mnemonic/name (string in tok_buf)
;   4 = TOK_DIR     directive without '.' (string in tok_buf)
;   5 = TOK_LABEL   label definition without ':' (string in tok_buf)
;   6 = TOK_COMMA   comma separator
;
; Symbol types:
;   0 = SYM_CONST   named constant (value = literal)
;   1 = SYM_LABEL   code label (value = code offset)
;   2 = SYM_PROC    procedure entry (value = code offset)
;   3 = SYM_GLOBAL  global variable (value = global segment offset)
;   4 = SYM_DATA    data block (value = data segment offset)
;
; Operand types (mnemonic table):
;   0 = NONE    1-byte instruction
;   1 = IMM8    2-byte: opcode + byte
;   2 = IMM24   4-byte: opcode + word
;   3 = D8_A24  5-byte: opcode + byte + word (calln)
;   4 = D8_O8   3-byte: opcode + byte + byte (loadn/storen)
;
; Register allocation:
;   r0 = work/scratch, parameter, return value
;   r1 = return address (jal) or scratch
;   r2 = scratch, function address for jal
;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR)
;
; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
;   RX ready = status bit 0

; ============================================================
; Entry point
; ============================================================
_start:
    ; Print boot message
    la r0, msg_boot
    la r2, uart_puts
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; Read all UART input into input_buf
    la r2, read_all_input
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; ---- Pass 1: collect symbols, compute sizes ----
    lc r0, 0
    la r2, input_pos
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Prime first character
    la r2, lex_advance
    jal r1, (r2)
    ; Set pass number = 1
    lc r0, 1
    la r2, pass_num
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    ; Reset counters
    lc r0, 0
    la r2, code_addr
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    la r2, global_offset
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    la r2, data_offset
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    la r2, sym_count
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Initialize name_pool_ptr
    la r0, name_pool
    la r2, name_pool_ptr
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Run pass 1
    la r2, parse_program
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; Save code size after pass 1
    la r2, code_addr
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    la r2, code_size
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Save data size
    la r2, data_offset
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    la r2, total_data_size
    sw r0, 0(r2)

    ; Patch data and global symbols with absolute offsets
    la r2, patch_symbols
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; ---- Pass 2: emit bytecode ----
    lc r0, 0
    la r2, input_pos
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Prime first character
    la r2, lex_advance
    jal r1, (r2)
    ; Set pass number = 2
    lc r0, 2
    la r2, pass_num
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    ; Initialize code output pointer
    la r0, code_buf
    la r2, code_ptr
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Initialize data output pointer
    la r0, data_buf
    la r2, data_ptr
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Run pass 2
    la r2, parse_program
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; ---- Dump bytecode for verification ----
    la r2, dump_bytecode
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; Done
    la r0, msg_done
    la r2, uart_puts
    jal r1, (r2)
halt_loop:
    bra halt_loop

; ============================================================
; UART helpers
; ============================================================

; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
; Leaf function. Clobbers: r0, r2. Preserves: r1.
uart_putc:
    push r0
    la r2, -65280

content.content:
1→; pvm.s — P-Code Virtual Machine for COR24
     2→;
     3→; Register allocation:
     4→;   r0 = W (work/scratch, opcode dispatch)
     5→;   r1 = scratch / return address for jal calls
     6→;   r2 = scratch
     7→;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR, used sparingly)
     8→;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
     9→;
    10→; VM state lives in memory struct (vm_state) because COR24 has only 3 GPRs.
    11→;
    12→; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
    13→;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
    14→;   RX ready = status bit 0
    15→; LED: port at -65024 (0xFF0200)
    16→;
    17→; COR24 ISA notes:
    18→;   lbu = load byte zero-extend, lb = load byte sign-extend
    19→;   ceq ra, rb sets C if ra == rb; cls ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (signed)
    20→;   clu ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (unsigned)
    21→;   brt/brf = branch if C true/false; bra = branch always
    22→;   jal r1, (r2) = r1 = PC+1, PC = r2 (call convention)
    23→;   jmp (r1) = return from jal call
    24→;   Valid load/store base registers: r0, r1, r2, fp (NOT sp)
    25→;   Can push/pop: r0, r1, r2, fp
    26→
    27→; ============================================================
    28→; Entry point
    29→; ============================================================
    30→_start:
    31→    ; Initialize VM state struct (fp = &vm_state)
    32→    la r0, vm_state
    33→    push r0
    34→    pop fp
    35→
    36→    ; pc = 0
    37→    lc r0, 0
    38→    sw r0, 0(fp)
    39→
    40→    ; esp = eval_stack base
    41→    la r0, eval_stack
    42→    sw r0, 3(fp)
    43→
    44→    ; csp = call_stack base

file.content:
; pvm.s — P-Code Virtual Machine for COR24
;
; Register allocation:
;   r0 = W (work/scratch, opcode dispatch)
;   r1 = scratch / return address for jal calls
;   r2 = scratch
;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR, used sparingly)
;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
;
; VM state lives in memory struct (vm_state) because COR24 has only 3 GPRs.
;
; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
;   RX ready = status bit 0
; LED: port at -65024 (0xFF0200)
;
; COR24 ISA notes:
;   lbu = load byte zero-extend, lb = load byte sign-extend
;   ceq ra, rb sets C if ra == rb; cls ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (signed)
;   clu ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (unsigned)
;   brt/brf = branch if C true/false; bra = branch always
;   jal r1, (r2) = r1 = PC+1, PC = r2 (call convention)
;   jmp (r1) = return from jal call
;   Valid load/store base registers: r0, r1, r2, fp (NOT sp)
;   Can push/pop: r0, r1, r2, fp

; ============================================================
; Entry point
; ============================================================
_start:
    ; Initialize VM state struct (fp = &vm_state)
    la r0, vm_state
    push r0
    pop fp

    ; pc = 0
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 0(fp)

    ; esp = eval_stack base
    la r0, eval_stack
    sw r0, 3(fp)

    ; csp = call_stack base
    la r0, call_stack
    sw r0, 6(fp)

    ; fp_vm = 0 (no frame yet)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 9(fp)

    ; gp = globals base
    la r0, globals_seg
    sw r0, 12(fp)

    ; hp = heap base
    la r0, heap_seg
    sw r0, 15(fp)

    ; code = code segment base
    la r0, code_seg
    sw r0, 18(fp)

    ; status = 0 (running)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 21(fp)

    ; trap_code = 0
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 24(fp)

    ; Print boot message
    la r0, msg_boot
    la r2, uart_puts
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; Enter VM main loop
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; ============================================================
; UART helpers
; ============================================================

; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
; Call via: jal r1, (r2) with r2 = uart_putc
; Clobbers: r0, r2 (r1 = return address, preserved)
uart_putc:
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
uart_putc_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_putc_wait
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    jmp (r1)

; uart_puts — print null-terminated string at address in r0
; Call via: jal r1, (r2) with r2 = uart_puts, r0 = string addr
; Clobbers: r0, r1, r2
uart_puts:
    push r1
    mov r1, r0
uart_puts_loop:
    lbu r0, 0(r1)
    ceq r0, z
    brt uart_puts_done
    push r1
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
uart_puts_tx:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_puts_tx
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    pop r1
    add r1, 1
    bra uart_puts_loop
uart_puts_done:
    pop r1
    jmp (r1)

; ============================================================
; VM fetch-decode-execute loop
; ============================================================
vm_loop:
    ; Check status: if not running (0), stop
    la r0, vm_state
    push r0
    pop fp
    lw r0, 21(fp)
    ceq r0, z
    brf vm_halted

    ; Fetch opcode byte from code[pc]
    lw r0, 18(fp)
    lw r2, 0(fp)
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = code_base + pc, use r0 as base register
    lbu r2, 0(r0)
    ; r2 = opcode byte (zero-extended)

    ; Increment pc
    lw r0, 0(fp)
    add r0, 1
    sw r0, 0(fp)

    ; Bounds check: opcode must be < 97 (0x00..0x60)
    mov r0, r2
    lc r2, 97
    clu r0, r2
    brt opcode_ok
    la r0, op_invalid
    jmp (r0)
opcode_ok:

    ; Dispatch: compute dispatch_table[opcode * 3]
    mov r2, r0

; ============================================================
; VM state struct (9 words = 27 bytes)
; ============================================================
vm_state:
    .word 0
    ; pc (offset 0)
    .word 0
    ; esp (offset 3)
    .word 0
    ; csp (offset 6)
    .word 0
    ; fp_vm (offset 9)
    .word 0
    ; gp (offset 12)
    .word 0
    ; hp (offset 15)
    .word 0
    ; code (offset 18)
    .word 0
    ; status (offset 21)
    .word 0
    ; trap_code (offset 24)

; ============================================================
; Memory segments
; ============================================================

; Test bytecode: trap handling
; Expected UART output: PVM OK\nOK\nTRAP 0\n
;
; Test 1: Print "OK\n" to confirm VM is running, then trigger user trap
;
;  0: push_s 79      02, 79        ; 'O'
;  2: sys 1          96, 1         ; PUTC
;  4: push_s 75      02, 75        ; 'K'
;  6: sys 1          96, 1         ; PUTC
;  8: push_s 10      02, 10        ; '\n'
; 10: sys 1          96, 1         ; PUTC
; 12: trap 0         54, 0         ; USER_TRAP (code 0)
; 14: sys 0          96, 0         ; HALT (should not reach here)
;
; Other trap tests (change bytecode to test each):
;   DIV_ZERO:       push_s 1, push_s 0, div  → TRAP 1
;   STACK_OVERFLOW: (fill stack past limit)   → TRAP 2
;   STACK_UNDERFLOW: drop (on empty stack)    → TRAP 3
;   INVALID_OPCODE: .byte 0xFF               → TRAP 4
;   NIL_POINTER:    push_s 0, load           → TRAP 6
code_seg:
    ; Print "OK\n"
    .byte 2, 79
    .byte 96, 1
    .byte 2, 75
    .byte 96, 1
    .byte 2, 10
    .byte 96, 1
    ; trap 0 (USER_TRAP) — opcode 0x36 = 54 decimal
    .byte 54, 0
    ; HALT (unreachable)
    .byte 96, 0

; Globals segment (8 words = 24 bytes)
globals_seg:
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0

; Call stack (grows upward, 96 bytes for nested frames)
call_stack:
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0
    .word 0

    .word 0
    .word 0

    .word 0
